items.description: Arabic numeral '7' in the upper right corner, outside the text block.
items.scholarly_note: Modern pagination or foliation mark added after the medieval period for reference and codex navigation.

items.description: The word 'Iþrir' written to the right of the main text block in a script resembling or variant from the primary Gothic hand.
items.scholarly_note: Possible early marginal gloss, stanza or section marker, or textual note. 'þrir' may reference 'three' or a variant reading; collation with other witnesses (e.g. AM 748 I 4to) required for interpretation.

items.description: The English word 'read' inscribed in a post-medieval Latin script in the right margin area.
items.scholarly_note: Modern annotation by a later reader, cataloguer, or scholar, likely indicating the page was examined or selected for study.

items.description: Large initial 'Þ' (thorn) with a markedly elongated vertical stem extending upward across multiple lines above the base of the letter.
items.scholarly_note: Scribal littera notabilior used to mark the start of a new section, poem division, or speaker attribution. The extended stem is a deliberate decorative and hierarchical feature typical of the Codex Regius main hand.

items.description: The sequence 'z v. ambroceir' at the end of the final line of text.
items.scholarly_note: 'z' is the standard abbreviation for 'ok' (and). 'v. ambroceir' may form part of the original prose conclusion or represent a later reference ('vide' + source or name); requires comparison with critical editions.

damage_notes: The examined photographic surrogate shows a clean, high-contrast surface with no visible holes, prominent scratches, or heavy staining.
In the original parchment (visible in project raw imagery), minor edge wear and age-related discoloration exist at the lower right and outer margins but remain minor and non-obstructive.
summary: Page 76 of Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to) presents a dense, legible block of Gothic book-hand text from the heroic poems with very few non-scribal additions. Visible elements outside or interrupting the main text are limited to a modern pagination numeral '7', two right-margin annotations of differing periods ('Iþrir' and 'read'), and a single large decorative initial 'Þ' with an extended stem marking a textual division. No figurative doodles, faces, animals, pen trials, interlinear insertions, or clear scribal corrections are visible. The surface in the provided image is clean and the leaf appears well-preserved with excellent legibility.
